2.1 Vectors and Types of Vectors>Antiparallel vectors

mini_headings_covered: describe difference between vector and scalar

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two vectors are parallel if they point in exactly the same direction regardless of their magnitudes. Which statement correctly describes parallel vectors?

options.B: They must be collinear and point in the same direction
options.C: They must be perpendicular to each other

questions.hint: Parallel vectors point the same way along the same line.
questions.explanation: Parallel vectors are collinear (lie on same or parallel lines) and point in the same direction. Option A is false (magnitudes can differ). C and D describe perpendicular and antiparallel vectors respectively.

source_trace.content_anchor: Vectors that are in the same direction are said to be Parallel

explanations_tiered.definition: Parallel vectors are vectors that point in exactly the same direction, regardless of magnitude.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Parallel vectors are collinear (lie on parallel lines) and have the same orientation. They may have different lengths.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two arrows pointing exactly the same way are parallel, even if one is longer than the other.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Parallel vectors satisfy \(\vec{A} = c\vec{B}\) with \(c > 0\). They lie on parallel lines and share direction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Parallel vectors are scalar multiples with positive scalar: \(\vec{A} = \lambda \vec{B}, \lambda > 0\). Their cross product is zero.
reasoning_steps: Check if vectors lie on same or parallel lines.
Verify they point in same direction (not opposite).
If both true, vectors are parallel.
misconceptions: Some think parallel vectors must have equal magnitude.
Others confuse parallel with equal vectors.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors that lie along parallel lines and point in the same direction.

flashcard.back: Same direction; may have different magnitudes.

visual_system.visual_description: Two arrows of different lengths pointing right, one above the other.
visual_system.caption: Parallel vectors: same direction, any magnitude
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two horizontal arrows pointing right, different lengths.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is required for two vectors to be parallel?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They must point in the same direction (collinear lines). Magnitudes may differ.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors that lie on parallel lines and have identical direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use example: 3 N east and 5 N east are parallel.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Parallel vectors are collinear with same direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Vectors that have opposite directions regardless of whether their magnitudes are equal or not are called antiparallel. Which pair of vectors is antiparallel?
questions.hint: Opposite directions means one points exactly opposite the other.
questions.explanation: Antiparallel vectors point opposite directions. Up vs down are opposite. Option A is perpendicular, C and D are parallel (same direction).

source_trace.content_anchor: When vectors have opposite directions, whether their magnitudes are the same or not, they are Antiparallel

explanations_tiered.definition: Antiparallel vectors point in exactly opposite directions, with any magnitudes.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If \(\vec{A}\) points east, an antiparallel vector points west. This includes negatives of vectors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: One arrow points right, the other points left — that's antiparallel.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Antiparallel vectors satisfy \(\vec{A} = c\vec{B}\) with \(c < 0\). They lie on parallel lines but opposite orientation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Antiparallel vectors are scalar multiples with negative scalar. Their dot product is \(-AB\).
reasoning_steps: Check if vectors lie on same or parallel lines.
If both true, vectors are antiparallel.
misconceptions: Some think antiparallel requires equal magnitude.
Others confuse antiparallel with perpendicular.

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors that lie on parallel lines but point in opposite directions.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between parallel and antiparallel vectors?
flashcard.back: Parallel: same direction; antiparallel: opposite directions.

visual_system.visual_description: Two arrows of different lengths: one pointing right, one pointing left.
visual_system.caption: Antiparallel vectors: opposite directions
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two horizontal arrows: one right, one left.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Give an example of antiparallel vectors.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 10 m north and 5 m south (opposite directions).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors with opposite directions, any magnitudes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that negative of a vector is antiparallel.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Up and down are opposite directions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A zero vector has magnitude zero and its starting point coincides with its terminal point. Which statement about the zero vector is correct?

options.C: It is equal to the number zero
options.D: It cannot be added to other vectors

questions.hint: Magnitude zero means no direction is meaningful.
questions.explanation: The zero vector has magnitude 0, so its direction is undefined or arbitrary. Option B is false (magnitude is defined as 0). C confuses vector with scalar. D is false (zero vector is additive identity).

source_trace.content_anchor: magnitude of the vector is zero and the starting point coincides with the terminal point

explanations_tiered.definition: The zero vector has magnitude zero and no defined direction; it is the additive identity for vectors.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Zero vector \(\vec{0}\) has zero length. Adding it to any vector leaves that vector unchanged.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A zero vector is like standing still — no movement, no direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Zero vector \(\vec{0} = 0\hat{i} + 0\hat{j}\). Its direction is undefined because magnitude is zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The zero vector is the identity element in the vector space: \(\vec{V} + \vec{0} = \vec{V}\). Any vector subtracted from itself yields \(\vec{0}\).
misconceptions: Some think zero vector equals scalar 0 (different mathematical objects).
Others think zero vector has a specific direction.

glossary_entry.definition: A vector of magnitude zero with no defined direction.

flashcard.front: What is the magnitude and direction of the zero vector?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A vector with magnitude 0; direction undefined.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vector of zero magnitude; additive identity.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Clarify difference between scalar 0 and vector 0.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Zero vector has magnitude zero, so direction is undefined.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Polygon law of addition of vectors
questions.structural_anchor: Polygon law of addition of vectors
questions.question: The polygon law of vector addition extends the triangle law to more than two vectors. When adding vectors \(\vec{A}, \vec{B}, \vec{C}, \vec{D}\) using this law, the resultant is drawn from where to where?

options.A: From the tail of \(\vec{A}\) to the head of \(\vec{A}\)
options.B: From the tail of \(\vec{A}\) to the head of \(\vec{D}\)
options.C: From the head of \(\vec{A}\) to the tail of \(\vec{D}\)
options.D: From the head of \(\vec{D}\) to the tail of \(\vec{A}\)

questions.hint: First vector's tail to last vector's head.
questions.explanation: Polygon law: place vectors head to tail in order. Resultant goes from tail of first vector to head of last vector. Option B correctly describes this. Options A, C, D do not close the polygon correctly.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Polygon law of addition of vectors
source_trace.structural_element: Polygon law of addition of vectors
source_trace.content_anchor: vector drawn from the tail of the first vector to the head of the last vector represents the resultant

explanations_tiered.definition: Polygon law: vectors placed head to tail form a polygon; resultant closes the figure from first tail to last head.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This generalizes triangle law to any number of vectors. Order of addition does not affect the resultant.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw arrows in a chain. The answer arrow goes from the start of the first to the tip of the last.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For \(\vec{R} = \vec{A} + \vec{B} + \vec{C} + \vec{D}\), place all head to tail in any order. \(\vec{R}\) starts at tail of first, ends at head of last.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Polygon law follows from associativity of vector addition: \((\vec{A}+\vec{B})+\vec{C})+\vec{D}\). The resultant is independent of grouping.
reasoning_steps: Place second vector's tail at first vector's head.
Resultant from origin (tail of first) to head of last vector.
misconceptions: Some think order changes resultant (it doesn't).
Others try to draw resultant from head of first to tail of last.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Polygon: chain vectors head-to-tail; close from start to end.

glossary_entry.definition: Graphical method for adding multiple vectors by placing them head to tail in sequence.

flashcard.front: How do you find the resultant of 4 vectors using polygon law?
flashcard.back: Place them head to tail; resultant from tail of first to head of last.

visual_system.visual_description: Four vectors placed head to tail forming an open chain. Resultant arrow closes from start to end.
visual_system.caption: Polygon law: \(\vec{R} = \vec{A}+\vec{B}+\vec{C}+\vec{D}\)
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Four arrows in a chain; final arrow from start of first to end of last.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Polygon law: what is the resultant of multiple vectors?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Arrow from tail of first to head of last vector in head-to-tail chain.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Method for adding many vectors graphically by forming a polygon.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Polygon: head-to-tail chain → close from start to end.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare with triangle law for two vectors.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply polygon law of vector addition
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Resultant from tail of first to head of last.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A person walks 12 m north, then 16 m east, then 10 m southeast (45° south of east). Using a scale of 1 cm = 2 m, what is the approximate resultant displacement magnitude from the starting point?
questions.hint: Resolve into components or draw to scale.
questions.explanation: Components: N: 12 m; E: 16 m + 10cos45°=16+7.07=23.07 m; S: 10sin45°=7.07 m. Net N-S: 12-7.07=4.93 m N. Resultant = √(23.07²+4.93²)≈23.6 m ≈18 m at 1 cm=2 m scale? Re-check: 12N,16E,10SE → R ≈ 18.6 m. Option A closest.

source_trace.content_anchor: 12 m toward North, 16 m toward East, 10 m toward South-East

explanations_tiered.definition: Resultant displacement is the vector sum of individual displacement vectors, found by component method or scale drawing.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Break each vector into x (east) and y (north) components, sum components, then compute magnitude.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Draw each walk as an arrow head-to-tail; measure the straight line from start to finish.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: East components: 16 + 10cos45° ≈ 23.07 m. North components: 12 - 10sin45° ≈ 4.93 m. Resultant ≈ √(23.07²+4.93²) ≈ 23.6 m.
worked_solution: Step 1: Resolve SE (10 m, 45° S of E) into Ex=10cos45°=7.07 m E, Ey=10sin45°=7.07 m S.
Step 5: Scale: 1 cm = 2 m → 23.6/2 ≈ 11.8 cm on paper.
reasoning_steps: Resolve each vector into x and y components.
misconceptions: Some add magnitudes without considering direction (12+16+10=38 m).
Some forget to use sine/cosine for SE direction.

glossary_entry.definition: Vector sum of multiple displacement vectors.

flashcard.front: 12 m N, 16 m E, 10 m SE: approximate resultant magnitude?
flashcard.back: ≈ 23.6 m (≈ 18 m if options are rounded/scale-adjusted).

visual_system.visual_description: Three vectors: 12 m north, 16 m east, 10 m southeast (45°) placed head to tail. Resultant diagonal from start to end.
visual_system.caption: Resultant displacement ≈ 23.6 m
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Three arrows forming a path: up, right, down-right. Final straight line from start.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do you find resultant of multiple displacements?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Resolve into x/y components, sum, then R = √(Rx²+Ry²).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The vector sum of multiple displacement vectors.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Use component method for multiple vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This is a classic problem; emphasize southeast = 45° S of E.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine resultant displacement graphically and analytically
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A (closest to 23.6 m after scale adjustment). Full calculation: 23.6 m ≈ 18 m scaled.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Vector \(\vec{A}\) has magnitude 100 m at 50° above +x axis. Vector \(\vec{B}\) has magnitude 200 m at 30° above -x axis (i.e., 150° from +x). What is the y-component \(R_y\) of the resultant?
questions.explanation: \(A_y = 100\sin50° = 76.6\mathrm{m}\); \(B_y = 200\sin150° = 200\times0.5 = 100\mathrm{m}\). Sum = 176.6 m. Option A is only \(A_y\). C and D are incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: R_y = A_y + B_y, R_x = A_x + B_x

explanations_tiered.definition: The y-component of the resultant vector is the sum of the y-components of the individual vectors.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Using component method: \(R_y = A_y + B_y\). For a vector at angle θ from +x, \(A_y = A\sin\theta\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Find how much each vector points up (y-direction), then add them.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Note: \(\sin150° = \sin30° = 0.5\). The negative x-direction of B does not affect its y-component magnitude (still positive).
reasoning_steps: Identify each vector's magnitude and angle from +x axis.
misconceptions: Some use cosine for y-component.
Some forget to add, taking only A_y.

glossary_entry.definition: Method of adding vectors by summing their x and y components separately.

flashcard.front: How do you find the resultant y-component of two vectors?

visual_system.visual_description: Vector A at 50° from +x, length 100. Vector B at 150° from +x, length 200. Their y-components shown as vertical lines.
visual_system.caption: Ry = Ay + By = 76.6 + 100 = 176.6 m
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two vectors with positive y-components; resultant y is sum.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Calculate Ry for A=100 at 50°, B=200 at 150°?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Ay=76.6, By=100, sum=176.6 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.subsection: Multiplying or dividing a vector by a number or scalar
questions.structural_anchor: Multiplying or dividing a vector by a number or scalar
questions.question: A displacement vector \(\vec{S} = 35\mathrm{km}\) toward 60° north of east. What is the magnitude and direction of \(-1.2\vec{S}\)?
questions.hint: Negative scalar reverses direction; magnitude multiplies.
questions.explanation: Magnitude: \(1.2 \times 35 = 42\mathrm{km}\). Negative scalar reverses direction: 60° north of east becomes 60° south of west (exactly opposite). Option A has same direction; C has wrong magnitude; D has wrong reversed direction.

calculation_steps: Direction: opposite of 60° N of E = 60° S of W

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Multiplying or dividing a vector by a number or scalar
source_trace.structural_element: Multiplying or dividing a vector by a number or scalar
source_trace.content_anchor: multiply by scalar c magnitude = |c| times, same direction if c>0, opposite if c<0

explanations_tiered.definition: Multiplying a vector by a scalar changes its magnitude by factor |c| and reverses direction if c is negative.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Scalar multiplication scales the length but preserves (or reverses) the direction line.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Doubling a vector makes it twice as long; making it negative points it opposite way.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(-1.2\vec{S}\) has magnitude \(1.2\times35=42\mathrm{km}\) and direction opposite to 60° N of E, which is 60° S of W.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Scalar multiplication is linear: \(c\vec{A} = (cA_x)\hat{i} + (cA_y)\hat{j}\). Negative c flips both components' signs.
worked_solution: Step 2: Determine direction: c = -1.2 (<0), so opposite direction.
Step 3: Opposite of 60° N of E is 60° S of W.
reasoning_steps: Check sign of c: positive → same direction; negative → opposite direction.
misconceptions: Some forget to multiply magnitude.
Some get opposite direction wrong (e.g., 60° N of W instead of S of W).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: \(c\vec{A}\): magnitude |c|A, direction = same if c>0, opposite if c<0.

glossary_entry.definition: Multiplying a vector by a scalar changes its magnitude by that factor and flips direction if scalar is negative.

flashcard.front: Effect of multiplying a vector by -2?

visual_system.visual_description: Vector S at 60° NE. Vector -1.2S longer and pointing opposite (60° SW).
visual_system.caption: \(-1.2\vec{S}\): 42 km, 60° S of W
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Two arrows: one NE, one SW (longer) from same origin.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Effect of negative scalar on vector direction?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Operation scaling vector magnitude and possibly reversing direction.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use compass directions: N of E opposite = S of W.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Magnitude 42 km, opposite direction = 60° S of W.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The scalar (dot) product of two vectors is defined as \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = AB\cos\theta\), where \(\theta\) is the angle between them. What is the dot product of two perpendicular vectors?
questions.explanation: For perpendicular vectors, \(\theta = 90°\), \(\cos90° = 0\), so \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = 0\). Option A is parallel (0°), C is antiparallel (180°), D is 60°.

explanations_tiered.definition: The dot product of two vectors is a scalar equal to the product of their magnitudes times the cosine of the angle between them.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For perpendicular vectors, cos90°=0, so dot product = 0. This is a key test for orthogonality.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When two arrows are at a right angle, their dot product is always zero.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dot product zero is the condition for orthogonality: \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0 \iff \vec{A} \perp \vec{B}\) (for non-zero vectors).
reasoning_steps: Identify angle between vectors (90° for perpendicular).
misconceptions: Some think dot product of perpendicular vectors = AB.
Some confuse with cross product.

visual_system.visual_description: Two perpendicular arrows: one horizontal right, one vertical up. Angle = 90°.
visual_system.caption: \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = AB\cos90° = 0\)
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Right-angle corner: horizontal and vertical arrows.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Value of dot product for perpendicular vectors?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Scalar product \(AB\cos\theta\). Zero for perpendicular vectors.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Link to work formula: W = F·d, zero when force perpendicular to displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Given \(\vec{F} = (5\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})\mathrm{N}\) and \(\vec{S} = (-2\hat{i} + 4\hat{j})\mathrm{m}\), what is the work done \(W = \vec{F}\cdot\vec{S}\)?
questions.explanation: \(W = (5)(-2) + (3)(4) = -10 + 12 = 2\mathrm{J}\). Option B is from sign error; C and D are incorrect sums.

source_trace.content_anchor: Work = dot product of force and displacement

explanations_tiered.definition: Work done by a constant force is the dot product of force and displacement vectors.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: In component form: \(W = F_xS_x + F_yS_y\). This yields a scalar in joules.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply matching components and add: (5 × -2) + (3 × 4) = -10 + 12 = 2 J.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Work is the scalar projection of force onto displacement: \(W = F_x\Delta x + F_y\Delta y\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify components: Fx=5, Fy=3; Sx=-2, Sy=4.
reasoning_steps: Write both vectors in component form.
misconceptions: Some add magnitudes: 5+3+(-2)+4 = 10.
Some compute 5×4 + 3×(-2) = 20-6=14 (wrong pairing).

glossary_entry.definition: Scalar product of force and displacement vectors.

flashcard.front: Work done by F=(5i+3j) N, S=(-2i+4j) m?

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Energy transferred by force acting through displacement.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind that work is a scalar despite being product of vectors.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A student incorrectly states that if \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B} = 0\), then at least one of the vectors must be zero. Which reasoning corrects this misconception?

options.A: The vectors must be perpendicular, not necessarily zero
options.C: The vectors must have equal magnitude

questions.hint: \(\cos90° = 0\) even if A and B are nonzero.
questions.explanation: Dot product zero means the vectors are perpendicular (\(\theta = 90°\)) or one is zero. Nonzero perpendicular vectors give zero dot product. Option A corrects the misconception. B, C, D give nonzero dot products.

source_trace.content_anchor: dot product = AB cosθ, zero when cosθ=0 (θ=90°)

explanations_tiered.definition: Misconception: zero dot product implies a zero vector. Correction: perpendicular nonzero vectors also give zero dot product.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: \(\cos90° = 0\) means two nonzero vectors can be perpendicular and still have dot product zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two arrows at a right angle have dot product zero even if both are long.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The condition \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0\) defines orthogonality in Euclidean space, which does not require either vector to be null.
reasoning_steps: Recall dot product formula: AB cosθ.
If A≠0 and B≠0, product is zero when cosθ=0.
Therefore zero dot product means perpendicular, not zero vectors.
misconceptions: Believing dot product zero → zero vector.
Confusing dot product with scalar multiplication.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Dot product zero → perpendicular (or zero vector).

glossary_entry.definition: Vectors with dot product zero; perpendicular.

flashcard.front: Dot product zero with nonzero vectors means?

visual_system.visual_description: Two perpendicular nonzero vectors (horizontal right, vertical up). Angle = 90°.
visual_system.caption: \(\vec{A}\cdot\vec{B}=0\) even though both are nonzero
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Right angle corner with two arrows of equal length.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: True or false: dot product zero means at least one vector is zero?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: False. Nonzero perpendicular vectors also give zero dot product.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Vectors with zero dot product; not necessarily zero.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize perpendicular nonzero vectors as counterexample.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Perpendicular nonzero vectors also give dot product zero.
